hits\nwork = df[df['hit'].notna()].copy()\nprint('rows', len(work), 'mean_hit', round(float(work['hit'].mean()), 6), 'mean_p_adj', round(float(work['p_adj'].mean()), 6), 'mean_p_cal', 'outputtelem/telemetry_calibration_upper_bucket_cooling_v1.json',\n    'outputtelem/telemetry_calibration_penalty_only_v1.json',\n]\n\ndef brier(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    return float(((p[m] - y[m]) ** 2).mean()) if m.any() else float('nan')\n\ndef logloss(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(1e-6, 1-1e-6)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    if not m.any():\n        return float('nan')\n    p = p[m]; y = y[m]\n    return float((-(y * np.log(p) + (1-y) * np.log(1-p))).mean())\n\nimport numpy as np\nrows = []\nfor rel in candidates:\n    p = root / rel\n    if not p.exists():\n        continue\n    obj = json.loads(p.read_text(encoding='utf-8'))\n    calib = TelemetryCalibration.from_json(obj)\n    overlay = apply_calibration_to_column(work, calib, source_col='p_for_cal', out_col='p_test', apply_under_penalty=True)\n    rows.append({\n        'file': rel,\n        'brier': brier(overlay['p_test'], overlay['hit']),\n        'logloss': logloss(overlay['p_test'], overlay['hit']),\n        'cal_applied': float(overlay.get('telemetry_cal_applied', pd.Series(dtype=bool)).mean()) if 'telemetry_cal_applied' in overlay.columns else None,\n        'k_shrink': obj.get('k_shrink'),\n        'under_penalty': obj.get('standard_under_penalty'),\n        'mult_n': len(obj.get('mult') or {}),\n        'on_n': len(obj.get('mult_rolectx_on') or {}),\n        'off_n': len(obj.get('mult_rolectx_off') or {}),\n    })\n\nrows = sorted(rows, key=lambda r: (r['brier'], r['logloss']))\nfor r in rows:\n    print(r)\n"

'line']\nkeep_cols = ['hit', 'p_adj', 'p_cal', 'p_for_cal', 'p_cal_src', 'role_ctx_outs_used', 'telemetry_cal_key', 'telemetry_mult', 'telemetry_under_penalty', 'telemetry_bucket_mult', 'telemetry_cal_applied']\nfor rd in run_dirs:\n    eval_df = pd.read_csv(rd / 'eval_legs.csv')\n    scored_df = pd.read_csv(rd / 'scored_legs_deduped.csv')\n    for c in key:\n        eval_df[c] = eval_df[c].astype(str).str.strip()\n        scored_df[c] = scored_df[c].astype(str).str.strip()\n    cols = [c for c in key + keep_cols if c in scored_df.columns]\n    merged = eval_df.merge(scored_df[cols].drop_duplicates(key, keep='first'), on=key, how='left', suffixes=('', '_scored'))\n    frames.append(merged)\n\ndf = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True)\nfor c in ['hit', 'p_adj', 'p_cal', 'p_for_cal', 'role_ctx_outs_used', 'telemetry_mult', 'telemetry_under_penalty', 'telemetry_bucket_mult']:\n    if c in df.columns:\n        df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors='coerce')\nif 'p_cal_src' not in df.columns:\n    df['p_cal_src'] = 'p_adj'\n\nwork = df[df['hit'].notna()].copy()\n# Rebuild a clean baseline where p_cal is the source probability to be calibrated.\nwork['p_cal'] = pd.to_numeric(work['p_for_cal'], errors='coerce').fillna(pd.to_numeric(work['p_adj'], errors='coerce')).clip(0.0, 1.0)\n\n\ndef brier(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    return float(((p[m] - y[m]) ** 2).mean()) if m.any() else float('nan')\n\ndef logloss(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(1e-6, 1-1e-6)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    if not m.any():\n        return float('nan')\n    p = p[m]; y = y[m]\n    return float((-(y * np.log(p) + (1-y) * np.log(1-p))).mean())\n\nbaseline_brier = brier(work['p_cal'], work['hit'])\nbaseline_logloss = logloss(work['p_cal'], work['hit'])\nprint('rows', len(work), 'baseline_brier', round(baseline_brier, 6), 'baseline_logloss', round(baseline_logloss, 6))\n\nfiles = [\n    'data/model/telemetry_calibration.json',\n    'data/model/telemetry_calibration.off.json',\n    'data/model/telemetry_calibration_shrink_0.88.json',\n    'data/model/telemetry_key_role_off_supplied_blend0.json',\n    'data/model/telemetry_key_role_off_trim_ast_pra_pts.json',\n    'data/model/telemetry_key_role_on_blend.json',\n    'data/model/telemetry_key_role_on_blend_0.05.json',\n    'data/model/telemetry_stat_direction_light.json',\n    'data/model/telemetry_stat_direction_rolectx_off.json',\n    'outputtelem/telemetry_calibration_targeted_lift_v1.json',\n    'outputtelem/telemetry_calibration_targeted_lift_v2.json',\n    'outputtelem/telemetry_calibration_targeted_lift_v3.json',\n    'outputtelem/telemetry_calibration_targeted_lift_v4_padj_only.json',\n    'outputtelem/telemetry_calibration_under_only_v1.json',\n    'outputtelem/telemetry_calibration_upper_bucket_cooling_v1.json',\n    'outputtelem/telemetry_calibration_penalty_only_v1.json',\n]\nrows = []\nfor rel in files:\n    p = root / rel\n    if not p.exists():\n        continue\n    obj = json.loads(p.read_text(encoding='utf-8'))\n    calib = TelemetryCalibration.from_json(obj)\n    tested = apply_calibration_to_column(work, calib, source_col='p_cal', out_col='p_test', apply_under_penalty=True)\n    rows.append({\n        'file': rel,\n        'brier': brier(tested['p_test'], tested['hit']),\n        'logloss': logloss(tested['p_test'], tested['hit']),\n        'delta_brier_vs_baseline': baseline_brier - brier(tested['p_test'], tested['hit']),\n        'delta_logloss_vs_baseline': baseline_logloss - logloss(tested['p_test'], tested['hit']),\n    })\n\nfor r in sorted(rows, key=lambda x: x['brier']):\n    print(r)\n"
}

Created memory file [](file:///c%3A/Users/rick/AppData/Roaming/Code/User/workspaceStorage/8813f013e83e6ff0ef695b9385f944bc/GitHub.copilot-chat/memory-tool/memories/MTI4ZWUwNGEtMTQ4NS00NWFjLTg5YjQtNjY2YTJhZThjY2Yz/telemetry_model_review.md)

I did not restore the model to identity.

What I found is that the live telemetry calibration is already the best calibration artifact we have for the fixed 20260318 role-off replay corpus, and the current blocker is the variant gate, not calibration promotion.

- The active live calibration path is config.yaml, loaded by telemetry_calibration.py, and applied in new_engine.py and calibration_map.py.
- The latest fixed corpus audit 20260318_190718/corpus_summary.md says `keep_identity`, but that means “do not promote a new calibration candidate,” not “revert the live model.”
- On the full 29-run replay set I compared the live calibration against the named telemetry files on disk. The live file improves Brier from about `0.214862` to `0.213363`, and no other on-disk calibration file I tested beat it.
- The earlier audit 20260318_121447/corpus_summary.md did show `stat_direction_light` as a calibration-only lead, but it was still blocked from promotion by the broader variant gates.

So the practical conclusion is:

- Keep telemetry_calibration.json as the live calibration for now.
- Do not swap it to identity or to one of the older named variants.
- Focus the next improvement pass on variant breadth and the failing slices, especially the `role_ctx_on` and `recent_third` regressions that are causing the promotion gate to fail.

If you want, I can next do one of these:
1. Trace the exact slice-level failure chain in the latest corpus and identify which stat/direction combinations are dragging `role_ctx_on`.
2. Build a new calibration candidate family from the current live file, tuned specifically for the weak slices, and test it against the fixed replay corpus.
3. Tighten the telemetry reader report so it clearly distinguishes “live calibration winner” from “promotable candidate,” which should make future audits much easier to reason about.

User: Do 1 & 2

how='left')\n    frames.append(merged)\n\ndf = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True)\nfor c in ['hit', 'p_adj', 'p_cal', 'p_for_cal', 'role_ctx_outs_used', 'telemetry_mult', 'telemetry_under_penalty', 'telemetry_bucket_mult']:\n    if c in df.columns:\n        df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors='coerce')\nwork = df[df['hit'].notna()].copy()\nwork['p_cal'] = pd.to_numeric(work['p_for_cal'], errors='coerce').fillna(0)\nwork['stat'] = work['stat'].astype(str).str.upper().str.strip()\nwork['direction'] = work['direction'].astype(str).str.upper().str.strip()\nwork['telemetry_cal_key'] = pd.to_numeric(cur['hit'], errors='coerce')\ncur['role_ctx_outs_used'] = pd.to_numeric(cur['role_ctx_outs_used'], errors='coerce').fillna(0)\n\n# compute per-slice deltas for role_ctx_on and recent_third and their overlap\nmask_on = cur['role_ctx_outs_used'] > 0\nmask_recent = cur['run_id'].astype(str).isin(sorted(cur['run_id'].astype(str).unique())[-max(2, len(cur['run_id'].astype(str).unique())//3):])\n# use the same recent_third as reader: last third of run_ids sorted lexicographically\nrun_ids = sorted(cur['run_id'].astype(str).unique())\nrecent_third_ids = run_ids[-max(2, len(run_ids)//3):]\nmask_recent = cur['run_id'].astype(str).isin(recent_third_ids)\n\nslices = {\n    'role_ctx_on': cur[mask_on].copy(),\n    'recent_third': cur[mask_recent].copy(),\n    'on_and_recent_third': cur[mask_on & mask_recent].copy(),\n}\n\ndef brier(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    return float(((p[m] - y[m]) ** 2).mean()) if m.any() else float('nan')\n\nfor name, sub in slices.items():\n    base = brier(sub['p_cal'], sub['hit'])\n    cand = brier(sub['p_cur'], sub['hit'])\n    print('\\nSLICE', name, 'rows', len(sub), 'base', round(base, 6), 'cand', round(cand, 6), 'delta', round(base-cand, 6))\n    rows = []\n    for k, grp in sub.groupby('telemetry_cal_key'):\n        b0 = brier(grp['p_cal'], grp['hit'])\n        b1 = brier(grp['p_cur'], grp['hit'])\n        rows.append((k, len(grp), b0-b1, b0, b1, float(grp['hit'].mean()), float(grp['p_cal'].mean()), float(grp['p_cur'].mean())))\n    rows.sort(key=lambda x: (x[2], x[1]))\n    print('worst 12 (most harmful first):')\n    for r in rows[:12]:\n        print(r)\n"

'telemetry_under_penalty', 'telemetry_bucket_mult', 'telemetry_cal_applied']\nfor rd in run_dirs:\n    eval_df = pd.read_csv(rd / 'eval_legs.csv')\n    scored_df = pd.read_csv(rd / 'scored_legs_deduped.csv')\n    for c in key:\n        eval_df[c] = eval_df[c].astype(str).str.strip()\n        scored_df[c] = scored_df[c].astype(str).str.strip()\n    cols = [c for c in key + keep_cols if c in scored_df.columns]\n    merged = eval_df.merge(scored_df[cols].drop_duplicates(key, keep='first'), on=key, how='left')\n    merged['run_id'] = rd.name\n    frames.append(merged)\n\ndf = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True)\nfor c in ['hit', 'p_adj', 'p_cal', 'role_ctx_outs_used']:\n    if c in df.columns:\n        df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors='coerce')\nwork = df[df['hit'].notna()].copy()\nif 'role_ctx_outs_used' in work.columns:\n    work['role_ctx_outs_used'] = pd.to_numeric(work['role_ctx_outs_used'], errors='coerce').fillna(0)\nelse:\n    work['role_ctx_outs_used'] = 0\nwork['stat'] = work['stat'].astype(str).str.upper().str.strip()\nwork['direction'] = work['direction'].astype(str).str.upper().str.strip()\nwork['telemetry_cal_key'] = work['stat'] + '|' + work['direction']\nif 'p_cal' not in work.columns:\n    work['p_cal'] = work['p_adj'] if 'p_adj' in work.columns else pd.Series(0.5, index=work.index)\nwork['p_cal'] = pd.to_numeric(work['p_cal'], errors='coerce').fillna(pd.to_numeric(work.get('p_adj', pd.Series(0.5, index=work.index)), errors='coerce')).clip(0.0, 1.0)\n\ncal = TelemetryCalibration.from_json(json.loads((root/'data/model/telemetry_calibration.json').read_text(encoding='utf-8')))\ncur = apply_calibration_to_column(work, cal, source_col='p_cal', out_col='p_cur', apply_under_penalty=True)\ncur['p_cur'] = pd.to_numeric(cur['p_cur'], errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\ncur['hit'] = pd.to_numeric(cur['hit'], errors='coerce')\n\nrun_ids = sorted(cur['run_id'].astype(str).unique())\nrecent_third_ids = run_ids[-max(2, len(run_ids)//3):]\nmask_on = cur['role_ctx_outs_used'] > 0\nmask_recent = cur['run_id'].astype(str).isin(recent_third_ids)\n\nslices = {\n    'role_ctx_on': cur[mask_on].copy(),\n    'recent_third': cur[mask_recent].copy(),\n    'on_and_recent_third': cur[mask_on & mask_recent].copy(),\n}\n\ndef brier(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    return float(((p[m] - y[m]) ** 2).mean()) if m.any() else float('nan')\n\nfor name, sub in slices.items():\n    base_b = brier(sub['p_cal'], sub['hit'])\n    cand_b = brier(sub['p_cur'], sub['hit'])\n    print('\\nSLICE', name, 'rows', len(sub), 'base', round(base_b, 6), 'cand', round(cand_b, 6), 'delta', round(base_b-cand_b, 6))\n    rows = []\n    for k, grp in sub.groupby('telemetry_cal_key'):\n        b0 = brier(grp['p_cal'], grp['hit'])\n        b1 = brier(grp['p_cur'], grp['hit'])\n        rows.append((k, len(grp), b0-b1, b0, b1, float(grp['hit'].mean()), float(grp['p_cal'].mean()), float(grp['p_cur'].mean())))\n    rows.sort(key=lambda x: (x[2], x[1]))\n    for r in rows[:15]:\n        print(r)\n"
}

The slice breakdown completed and is in a temp result file. I’m reading it now, then I’ll turn the worst offenders into a small candidate family and score those variants on the same replay set.

    b0 = brier(grp['p_cal'], grp['hit'])\n        b1 = brier(grp['p_cur'], grp['hit'])\n        rows.append((k, len(grp), b0-b1, b0, b1, float(grp['hit'].mean()), float(grp['p_cal'].mean()), Ran `pylanceRunCodeSnippet` 
Completed with input: {
  "workspaceRoot": "C:\\Users\\rick\\projects\\Atlas",
  "workingDirectory": "C:\\Users\\rick\\projects\\Atlas",
  "timeout": 120000,
  "codeSnippet": "from pathlib import Path\nimport json\nimport pandas as pd\nimport numpy as np\nfrom Atlas.runtime.telemetry_calibration import TelemetryCalibration, apply_calibration_to_column\n\nroot = Path(r'C:\\Users\\rick\\projects\\Atlas')\nbase = root / 'outputtelem' / 'role_off_full_20260318_extracted'\nrun_dirs = []\nfor eval_path in sorted(base.rglob('eval_legs.csv')):\n    if eval_path.parent.name in {'System', 'Windfall'}:\n        continue\n    if (eval_path.with_name('scored_legs_deduped.csv')).exists():\n        run_dirs.append(eval_path.parent)\n\nframes = []\nkey = ['player', 'stat', 'direction', 'line']\nscore_cols = ['p_adj', 'p_cal_src', 'role_ctx_outs_used', 'hit']\nfor rd in run_dirs:\n    eval_df = pd.read_csv(rd / 'eval_legs.csv')\n    scored_df = pd.read_csv(rd / 'scored_legs_deduped.csv')\n    for c in key:\n        eval_df[c] = eval_df[c].astype(str).str.strip()\n        scored_df[c] = scored_df[c].astype(str).str.strip()\n    cols = [c for c in key + score_cols if c in scored_df.columns]\n    merged = eval_df.merge(scored_df[cols].drop_duplicates(key, keep='first'), on=key, how='left')\n    merged['run_id'] = rd.name\n    frames.append(merged)\n\ndf = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True)\nfor c in ['hit', 'p_adj', 'role_ctx_outs_used']:\n    if c in df.columns:\n        df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors='coerce')\nwork = df[df['hit'].notna()].copy()\nwork['role_ctx_outs_used'] = pd.to_numeric(work['role_ctx_outs_used'], errors='coerce').fillna(0) if 'role_ctx_outs_used' in work.columns else 0\nwork['stat'] = work['stat'].astype(str).str.upper().str.strip()\nwork['direction'] = work['direction'].astype(str).str.upper().str.strip()\nwork['telemetry_cal_key'] = work['stat'] + '|' + work['direction']\nwork['p_adj'] = pd.to_numeric(work['p_adj'], errors='coerce').fillna(0.5).clip(0.0, 1.0)\nif 'p_cal_src' not in work.columns:\n    work['p_cal_src'] = 'p_adj'\nwork['p_cal_src'] = work['p_cal_src'].astype(str)\n\nbase_cal = json.loads((root/'data/model/telemetry_calibration.json').read_text(encoding='utf-8'))\n\n\ndef scaled_cal(alpha_on: float, alpha_off: float, under_penalty: float = 0.98):\n    obj = json.loads(json.dumps(base_cal))\n    if 'mult_rolectx_on' in obj:\n        obj['mult_rolectx_on'] = {k: round(1.0 + alpha_on * (float(v) - 1.0), 10) for k, v in obj['mult_rolectx_on'].items()}\n    if 'mult_rolectx_off' in obj:\n        obj['mult_rolectx_off'] = {k: round(1.0 + alpha_off * (float(v) - 1.0), 10) for k, v in obj['mult_rolectx_off'].items()}\n    obj['standard_under_penalty'] = under_penalty\n    return TelemetryCalibration.from_json(obj), obj\n\n\ndef brier(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    return float(((p[m] - y[m]) ** 2).mean()) if m.any() else float('nan')\n\ndef logloss(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(1e-6, 1-1e-6)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    if not m.any():\n        return float('nan')\n    p = p[m]; y = y[m]\n    return float((-(y * np.log(p) + (1-y) * np.log(1-p))).mean())\n\nrun_ids = sorted(work['run_id'].astype(str).unique())\nrecent_third_ids = run_ids[-max(2, len(run_ids)//3):]\nmask_on = work['role_ctx_outs_used'] > 0\nmask_recent = work['run_id'].astype(str).isin(recent_third_ids)\n\ncandidate_specs = [\n    ('current_live', 1.0, 1.0, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.75', 0.75, 0.75, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.50', 0.50, 0.50, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.25', 0.25, 0.25, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.50_up1.0', 0.50, 0.50, 1.00),\n    ('roleon_only_0.50', 0.50, 1.00, 0.98),\n]\nresults = []\nfor name, a_on, a_off, up in candidate_specs:\n    cal, raw = scaled_cal(a_on, a_off, under_penalty=up)\n    tested = apply_calibration_to_column(work, cal, source_col='p_adj', out_col='p_test', apply_under_penalty=True)\n    tested['p_test'] = pd.to_numeric(tested['p_test'], errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\n    tested['hit'] = pd.to_numeric(tested['hit'], errors='coerce')\n    results.append({\n        'name': name,\n        'alpha_on': a_on,\n        'alpha_off': a_off,\n        'under_penalty': up,\n        'overall_brier': brier(tested['p_test'], tested['hit']),\n        'overall_logloss': logloss(tested['p_test'], tested['hit']),\n        'role_ctx_on_brier': brier(tested.loc[mask_on, 'p_test'], tested.loc[mask_on, 'hit']),\n        'role_ctx_on_delta': brier(tested.loc[mask_on, 'p_adj'], tested.loc[mask_on, 'hit']) - brier(tested.loc[mask_on, 'p_test'], tested.loc[mask_on, 'hit']),\n        'recent_third_brier': brier(tested.loc[mask_recent, 'p_test'], tested.loc[mask_recent, 'hit']),\n        'recent_third_delta': brier(tested.loc[mask_recent, 'p_adj'], tested.loc[mask_recent, 'hit']) - brier(tested.loc[mask_recent, 'p_test'], tested.loc[mask_recent, 'hit']),\n        'on_recent_brier': brier(tested.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'p_test'], tested.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'hit']),\n    })\n\nfor r in sorted(results, key=lambda x: x['overall_brier']):\n    print(r)\n"
}

run_dirs.append(eval_path.parent)\n\nframes = []\nkey = ['player', 'stat', 'direction', 'line']\nscore_cols = ['p_cal', 'p_cal_src', 'role_ctx_outs_used', 'hit']\nfor rd in run_dirs:\n    eval_df = how='left')\n    merged['run_id'] = rd.name\n    frames.append(merged)\n\ndf = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True)\nfor c in ['hit', 'p_cal', 'role_ctx_outs_used']:\n    if c in df.columns:\n        df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors='coerce')\nwork = df[df['hit'].notna()].copy()\nwork['role_ctx_outs_used'] = pd.to_numeric(work['role_ctx_outs_used'], errors='coerce').fillna(0) if 'role_ctx_outs_used' in work.columns else 0\nwork['stat'] = work['stat'].astype(str).str.upper().str.strip()\nwork['direction'] = work['direction'].astype(str).str.upper().str.strip()\nwork['telemetry_cal_key'] = work['stat'] + '|' + work['direction']\nwork['p_cal'] = pd.to_numeric(work['p_cal'], errors='coerce').fillna(0.5).clip(0.0, 1.0)\nif 'p_cal_src' not in work.columns:\n    work['p_cal_src'] = 'p_adj'\nwork['p_cal_src'] = work['p_cal_src'].astype(str)\n\nbase_cal = json.loads((root/'data/model/telemetry_calibration.json').read_text(encoding='utf-8'))\n\n\ndef make_cal(alpha_on, alpha_off, under_penalty):\n    obj = json.loads(json.dumps(base_cal))\n    if 'mult_rolectx_on' in obj:\n        obj['mult_rolectx_on'] = {k: round(1.0 + alpha_on * (float(v) - 1.0), 10) for k, v in obj['mult_rolectx_on'].items()}\n    if 'mult_rolectx_off' in obj:\n        obj['mult_rolectx_off'] = {k: round(1.0 + alpha_off * (float(v) - 1.0), 10) for k, v in obj['mult_rolectx_off'].items()}\n    obj['standard_under_penalty'] = under_penalty\n    return TelemetryCalibration.from_json(obj)\n\n\ndef brier(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    return float(((p[m] - y[m]) ** 2).mean()) if m.any() else float('nan')\n\n\ndef logloss(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(1e-6, 1-1e-6)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    if not m.any():\n        return float('nan')\n    p = p[m]; y = y[m]\n    return float((-(y * np.log(p) + (1-y) * np.log(1-p))).mean())\n\nrun_ids = sorted(work['run_id'].astype(str).unique())\nrecent_third_ids = run_ids[-max(2, len(run_ids)//3):]\nmask_on = work['role_ctx_outs_used'] > 0\nmask_recent = work['run_id'].astype(str).isin(recent_third_ids)\n\ncandidates = [\n    ('current_live', 1.0, 1.0, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.75', 0.75, 0.75, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.50', 0.50, 0.50, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.25', 0.25, 0.25, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.50_up1.0', 0.50, 0.50, 1.00),\n    ('roleon_only_0.50', 0.50, 1.00, 0.98),\n]\nresults = []\nfor name, a_on, a_off, up in candidates:\n    cal = make_cal(a_on, a_off, up)\n    tested = apply_calibration_to_column(work, cal, source_col='p_cal', out_col='p_test', apply_under_penalty=True)\n    tested['p_test'] = pd.to_numeric(tested['p_test'], errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\n    tested['hit'] = pd.to_numeric(tested['hit'], errors='coerce')\n    results.append({\n        'name': name,\n        'overall_brier': brier(tested['p_test'], tested['hit']),\n        'overall_logloss': logloss(tested['p_test'], tested['hit']),\n        'role_ctx_on_delta': brier(tested.loc[mask_on, 'p_cal'], tested.loc[mask_on, 'hit']) - brier(tested.loc[mask_on, 'p_test'], tested.loc[mask_on, 'hit']),\n        'recent_third_delta': brier(tested.loc[mask_recent, 'p_cal'], tested.loc[mask_recent, 'hit']) - brier(tested.loc[mask_recent, 'p_test'], tested.loc[mask_recent, 'hit']),\n        'on_recent_delta': brier(tested.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'p_cal'], tested.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'hit']) - brier(tested.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'p_test'], tested.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'hit']),\n    })\n\nfor r in sorted(results, key=lambda x: x['overall_brier']):\n    print(r)\n"

brier(out['p_test'], out['hit']),\n        'role_ctx_on_delta': brier(out.loc[mask_on, 'p_base_before'], out.loc[mask_on, 'hit']) - brier(out.loc[mask_on, 'p_test'], out.loc[mask_on, 'hit']),\n        'recent_third_delta': brier(out.loc[mask_recent, 'p_base_before'], out.loc[mask_recent, 'hit']) - brier(out.loc[mask_recent, 'p_test'], out.loc[mask_recent, 'hit']),\n        'on_recent_delta': brier(out.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'p_base_before'], out.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'hit']) - brier(out.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'p_test'], out.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'hit']),\n    })\n\nfor r in sorted(results, key=lambda x: x['overall_brier']):\n    print(r)\n"

  "codeSnippet": "from pathlib import Path\nimport json\nimport pandas as pd\nimport numpy as np\nfrom Atlas.runtime.telemetry_calibration import TelemetryCalibration, apply_calibration_to_column\n\nroot = Path(r'C:\\Users\\rick\\projects\\Atlas')\nbase = root / 'outputtelem' / 'role_off_full_20260318_extracted'\nrun_dirs = [p.parent for p in sorted(base.rglob('eval_legs.csv')) if p.parent.name not in {'System', 'Windfall'}]\nframes = []\nfor rd in run_dirs:\n    df = pd.read_csv(rd / 'eval_legs.csv')\n    df['run_id'] = rd.name\n    frames.append(df)\nwork = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True)\nfor c in ['hit', 'p_cal', 'p_for_cal', 'role_ctx_outs_used']:\n    work[c] = pd.to_numeric(work[c], errors='coerce')\nwork = work[work['hit'].notna()].copy()\nwork['stat'] = work['stat'].astype(str).str.upper().str.strip()\nwork['direction'] = work['direction'].astype(str).str.upper().str.strip()\nwork['telemetry_cal_key'] = work['stat'] + '|' + work['direction']\nwork['p_cal_src'] = work['p_cal_src'].astype(str)\n\nbase_cal = json.loads((root/'data/model/telemetry_calibration.json').read_text(encoding='utf-8'))\n\ndef make_cal(alpha_on, alpha_off, under_penalty):\n    obj = json.loads(json.dumps(base_cal))\n    obj['mult_rolectx_on'] = {k: round(1.0 + alpha_on * (float(v) - 1.0), 10) for k, v in obj['mult_rolectx_on'].items()}\n    obj['mult_rolectx_off'] = {k: round(1.0 + alpha_off * (float(v) - 1.0), 10) for k, v in obj['mult_rolectx_off'].items()}\n    obj['standard_under_penalty'] = under_penalty\n    return TelemetryCalibration.from_json(obj)\n\ndef brier(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    return float(((p[m] - y[m]) ** 2).mean()) if m.any() else float('nan')\n\ndef logloss(p, y):\n    p = pd.to_numeric(p, errors='coerce').clip(1e-6, 1-1e-6)\n    y = pd.to_numeric(y, errors='coerce')\n    m = p.notna() & y.notna()\n    if not m.any():\n        return float('nan')\n    p = p[m]; y = y[m]\n    return float((-(y * np.log(p) + (1-y) * np.log(1-p))).mean())\n\nrun_ids = sorted(work['run_id'].astype(str).unique())\nrecent_third_ids = run_ids[-max(2, len(run_ids)//3):]\nmask_on = work['role_ctx_outs_used'] > 0\nmask_recent = work['run_id'].astype(str).isin(recent_third_ids)\n\ncandidates = [\n    ('current_live', 1.0, 1.0, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.75', 0.75, 0.75, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.50', 0.50, 0.50, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.25', 0.25, 0.25, 0.98),\n    ('soft_0.50_up1.0', 0.50, 0.50, 1.00),\n    ('roleon_only_0.50', 0.50, 1.00, 0.98),\n]\nresults = []\nfor name, a_on, a_off, up in candidates:\n    cal = make_cal(a_on, a_off, up)\n    scored = work.copy()\n    scored['p_source_before'] = scored['p_for_cal']\n    out = apply_calibration_to_column(scored, cal, source_col='p_for_cal', out_col='p_test', apply_under_penalty=True)\n    out['p_test'] = pd.to_numeric(out['p_test'], errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\n    out['p_source_before'] = pd.to_numeric(out['p_source_before'], errors='coerce').clip(0.0, 1.0)\n    out['hit'] = pd.to_numeric(out['hit'], errors='coerce')\n    results.append({\n        'name': name,\n        'overall_brier': brier(out['p_test'], out['hit']),\n        'overall_logloss': logloss(out['p_test'], out['hit']),\n        'delta_vs_base': brier(out['p_source_before'], out['hit']) - brier(out['p_test'], out['hit']),\n        'role_ctx_on_delta': brier(out.loc[mask_on, 'p_source_before'], out.loc[mask_on, 'hit']) - brier(out.loc[mask_on, 'p_test'], out.loc[mask_on, 'hit']),\n        'recent_third_delta': brier(out.loc[mask_recent, 'p_source_before'], out.loc[mask_recent, 'hit']) - brier(out.loc[mask_recent, 'p_test'], out.loc[mask_recent, 'hit']),\n        'on_recent_delta': brier(out.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'p_source_before'], out.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'hit']) - brier(out.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'p_test'], out.loc[mask_on & mask_recent, 'hit']),\n    })\n\nfor r in sorted(results, key=lambda x: x['overall_brier']):\n    print(r)\n"
}

**Summary**
- The work started with a deep review request for the telemetry corpus under \.atlas_audit/diagnostics/telemetry_corpus/20260318_121447 and the related calibration files, with the goal of improving the model rather than reverting it.
- I inspected the corpus reader and runtime calibration path in telemetry_corpus_reader.py and telemetry_calibration.py, plus the live calibration inputs in telemetry_calibration.json and related candidate files.
- The first pass through the audits showed that the existing candidates still did not clear promotion gates, so I stopped trying to restore identity after you explicitly said you wanted the model improved instead.
- The analysis shifted to slice-level failure diagnosis on the fixed replay corpus, especially the weak slices `role_ctx_on`, `recent_third`, and their overlap.

**What Was Found**
- The latest failure chain is concentrated in over-direction cells, especially `RA|OVER`, `PA|OVER`, `PR|OVER`, `PRA|OVER`, `REB|OVER`, `AST|OVER`, and `PTS|OVER`.
- The most recent slice deltas I computed were:
- `role_ctx_on`: 10,418 rows, baseline Brier 0.25, candidate Brier 0.250184, delta -0.000184.
- `recent_third`: 19,832 rows, baseline Brier 0.25, candidate Brier 0.253023, delta -0.003023.
- `on_and_recent_third`: 3,080 rows, baseline Brier 0.25, candidate Brier 0.250207, delta -0.000207.
- The earlier audit summary at \.atlas_audit/diagnostics/telemetry_corpus/20260318_190718 still recommended `keep_identity`, with `telemetry_key_role_off_light` as the best named candidate but still not promotable.

**Most Recent Commands And Results**
- I reran candidate-family scoring against the replay corpus using the live calibration JSON as the starting point.
- The first scoring pass exposed a merge/source-shape issue, so I checked the replay CSV headers and confirmed that eval_legs.csv already contains the needed lineage and outcome columns, including `p_for_cal`, `p_cal`, `p_cal_src`, `hit`, and the telemetry fields.
- That made it clear that merging `scored_legs_deduped.csv` into the replay path was unnecessary and could hide the actual source probability by duplicating column names.
- I updated the session note with that lesson so the next pass does not repeat the same no-op calibration comparison.

**Latest Candidate Scoring**
- I tested a small family of softened variants derived from the current live calibration:
- `soft_0.50` was the best of the variants I tried on overall Brier, at 0.21286735007986007.
- `soft_0.25` and `soft_0.75` were close behind.
- The current live configuration in that pass was at 0.21306802824492255.
- The softening variants improved overall score relative to the live reference, but the slice-delta readout still needs one more careful pass because the calibration helper’s source handling can make some comparisons look like a no-op if the wrong source column is used.

**Current State**
- The main technical conclusion is not that the model is irreparable. It is that the strongest remaining weakness is concentrated and measurable, and the next useful step is still candidate-family tuning around those weak slices.
- The main blocker right now is not the reader logic itself. It is making sure the candidate family is scored from the intended source column so the slice gains and regressions are measured correctly.
- The conversation is at the point where the failure chain is understood, the existing candidates are not good enough, and the next pass should focus on a better tuned calibration family rather than restoring identity.

User: Ok! So what would you recommend the plan be?

GitHub Copilot: I’d recommend this plan:

1. Lock the evaluation path first.
- Treat eval_legs.csv as the canonical replay input for calibration scoring.
- Do not rely on a merge with scored_legs_deduped.csv unless a specific missing field is required.
- Preserve `p_for_cal`, `p_cal_src`, and the outcome columns so the calibration overlay is measured against the true source probability, not a transformed or duplicated one.

2. Build a small, controlled candidate family from telemetry_calibration.json.
- Keep the current live calibration as the baseline.
- Generate softened variants that move the strongest multipliers toward neutral in a few steps, for example 0.75, 0.50, and 0.25 strength.
- Test `standard_under_penalty` both at the current value and at 1.0.
- Keep role-context handling separate for `mult_rolectx_on` and `mult_rolectx_off` so you can see which side actually helps.

3. Score each candidate on the fixed replay corpus.
- Rank by overall Brier first.
- Then check slice deltas for `role_ctx_on`, `recent_third`, and `on_and_recent_third`.
- Also watch the worst cells we already identified: `RA|OVER`, `PA|OVER`, `PR|OVER`, `PRA|OVER`, `REB|OVER`, `AST|OVER`, and `PTS|OVER`.

4. Promote only if it clearly beats the live file and does not trade off the weak slices.
- If a candidate improves overall Brier but still worsens `recent_third`, do not promote it yet.
- If nothing clears the slice guards, narrow the candidate family to only the specific harmful cells rather than softening the whole calibration.

5. If the whole family still stalls, do a targeted cell-level adjustment pass.
- Focus on the exact over-direction cells driving the regressions.
- Avoid broad changes to unrelated keys.
- Re-run the same fixed corpus after each adjustment so the comparison stays stable.

My recommendation is: do not revert to identity, do not promote the current live file yet, and next focus on a tightly controlled softened family centered on the weak slices.

A gate-passing combo is possible in theory, but I would not bet on a broad blend of the families we’ve already tested.

What the data says:
- The best current candidates are still only clearing 1 of 10 calibration slices.
- The new softening families and weak-over targeted families improved the search space, but they did not move `recent_third` or `role_ctx_on` enough to matter for the gate.
- Because the gate requires at least 70% pass share with no severe regressions, we need a much more surgical improvement than a simple average of existing candidates.

What I think is the only plausible combo worth trying next:
- Keep the stronger existing role-off telemetry-key shape from `telemetry_key_role_off_light`.
- Add a very small correction layer only on:
- `role_ctx_on`
- `recent_third`
- the worst over keys: `RA|OVER`, `PA|OVER`, `PR|OVER`, `PRA|OVER`, `REB|OVER`, `AST|OVER`, `PTS|OVER`

Why that combo is the only credible one:
- It gives you the broadest existing improvement without throwing away the current good behavior.
- It also attacks the two weak slices that are still dragging the model.
- A generic blend or full softening is unlikely to pass, because we already tested those directions and they stayed below the gate.

So my answer is:
- Yes, a unique combination could exist.
- No, I do not think a broad mixture will pass.
- The only candidate worth pursuing is a hybrid: role-off telemetry-key structure plus a very narrow role_ctx_on/recent_third overlay.

If you want, I can do that next and build the hybrid candidate family directly.

Made changes.

User: Maybe instead of trying a hybrid approach we set a whole new schema of calibration and yaml files. The config.yaml likely has alot to do with the ceiling also. Just more suggestions

GitHub Copilot: Yes. At this point I would stop trying to squeeze more out of one calibration payload and move to a schema-driven setup.

My recommendation:

1. Split calibration into layers.
- Keep the numeric probability transform in one file family.
- Move policy and gating decisions into config.yaml.
- Treat them as separate concerns: calibration says how to adjust probabilities, config says when and where that adjustment is allowed.

2. Introduce a versioned calibration schema.
- Add an explicit schema version field.
- Add named families for:
- role-off telemetry key corrections
- role-on corrections
- recent-third corrections
- weak-over corrections
- optional hybrid overlays
- That gives you room to test combinations without overloading one JSON shape.

3. Make config.yaml control the ceiling.
- The current ceiling is likely being limited by global defaults like min count, deviation caps, pooling strength, and apply-only prefixes.
- I would expose those as experiment knobs in config, not hardcode them in the calibration file.
- That lets you search for a better policy without rewriting the whole payload each time.

4. Separate base calibration from correction overlays.
- Base layer: stable, conservative, broad behavior.
- Overlay layer: narrow corrections for weak slices only.
- This is cleaner than trying to force one file to explain all contexts.

5. Make the candidate search explicit in config.
- Add named candidate families and enable them through config.
- That way you can test matrices like:
- base only
- base plus role-off overlay
- base plus recent-third overlay
- base plus role-on overlay
- base plus weak-over overlay
- base plus combinations
- This is better than implicit blending because you can compare the combinations directly.

6. Tighten the gate policy separately from the model.
- Right now the calibration candidates are not failing because the idea is wrong.
- They are failing because the gates are conservative and the slices are still weak.
- If the model family changes, the gate thresholds should still be explicit and configurable.

What I would do next:
1. Define a new calibration schema version.
2. Add a config section for calibration policy and family selection.
3. Encode role-off, role-on, and weak-over overlays as separate named families.
4. Re-run the corpus against a small matrix of config plus calibration combinations.

My view is that this is more promising than another hybrid tweak. The current family is near the ceiling, so a schema and policy split is the more scalable path.

If you want, I can draft the new schema and config layout next.

I understand the model as a layered probability system, not a single scorer.

- `p_role` is the role-context-adjusted surface when `role_ctx_outs_used > 0`
- `p_cal` is the calibrated surface the engine actually ships
- `role_ctx` is a gating/context feature, not the end goal by itself
- `brier`, `logloss`, and ECE measure calibration quality
- `legloss` and slip outcomes measure whether the calibrated probabilities are producing better slip selections downstream

The key runtime path is in main.py and the late overlay happens in telemetry_calibration.py. The audit metrics and promotion logic live in telemetry_corpus_reader.py.

So the practical target is not just “make one column smaller on Brier.” It is:
- improve the upstream surface selection between `p_adj` and `p_role`
- improve the calibration overlay policy that transforms `p_cal`
- keep `role_ctx` useful without letting it distort the wrong rows
- then check whether slip quality actually improves, not just probability fit

I also recorded the important constraint in session memory: telemetry overlays late, and `p_role` only feeds `p_cal` when role context is active. That matters because it means calibration work has to respect the upstream surface choice rather than treating everything as one probability column.

If you want, the next useful step is a layer-by-layer audit with these buckets:
1. `p_role` quality
2. `p_cal` overlay quality
3. `role_ctx` segmentation quality
4. downstream slip ranking quality

That will tell us whether the next improvement should be in the model surface, the calibration policy, or the slip builder.

**Layer 1: Raw probability surface**
- The current live telemetry overlay is only a small gain over the underlying surface: Brier improves from 0.210134 to 0.209817 and logloss from 0.607355 to 0.606517 in corpus_summary.json.
- That tells me the model is not broken at the base-probability level, but the available calibration room is modest.
- The audit also shows 72.6336% of rows stayed unchanged by telemetry, so most of the surface is still coming through untouched.

**Layer 2: Role context**
- This is the main bottleneck.
- `role_ctx_off` is consistently positive, while `role_ctx_on` is consistently negative. In the best candidate, `role_ctx_off` has delta Brier +0.000661, but `role_ctx_on` is -0.000472 with a severe regression in candidate_scores.json.
- Identity is even worse on `role_ctx_on`, which means the branch itself is fragile, not just the calibration overlay.
- The strongest interpretation is that `role_ctx_off` is the cleaner surface and `role_ctx_on` needs separate treatment instead of shared global tuning.

**Layer 3: Time-window stability**
- `recent_third` is the repeated weak slice.
- The best calibration candidate only gets about +0.000014 on that slice, while several shrink and under-penalty candidates regress it in candidate_scores.json.
- That means the model is not yet learning a stable recent-window rule. If we keep using a recent-third concept, it probably needs to remain audit-only unless we expose a stable runtime proxy.

**Layer 4: Calibration policy**
- The best candidate in the current audit is `stat_direction_light`, with Brier 0.209374 and ECE 0.017343, but it still fails the overall gate because `role_ctx_on` and `recent_third` regressions block promotion in candidate_scores.json.
- The recommendation layer says to keep calibration and calibration-map logic as-is, which means the corpus is not asking for structural code surgery yet in logic_recommendations.json.
- The proposed calibration mode is still `keep_identity` in proposed_calibration.json, which means the audit sees a lead, but not a promotable one.

**Layer 5: Slip construction**
- The slip layer is not the first bottleneck.
- The config recommendations say no apply-now changes, and the variant score is dominated by stability penalty rather than raw realized edge in config_recommendations.json.
- In other words, the slip builder is reacting to uncertainty upstream, not missing a simple beam-width tweak.
- This is consistent with the engine flow in main.py and the late calibration overlay in telemetry_calibration.py.

**Bottom line**
- The cleanest path forward is:
- Keep `role_ctx_off` as the trusted surface.
- Split `role_ctx_on` into its own family or gating logic instead of sharing the same global calibration behavior.
- Treat `recent_third` as unstable until there is a stable runtime proxy.
- Only after that should we expect slip-level changes to matter materially.

I also recorded the key takeaway in session memory: `role_ctx_off` is the only consistently positive calibration slice, while `role_ctx_on` and `recent_third` are the recurring regressions, and slip ranking is mostly absorbing stability penalty.

Next, I can do one of two focused audits:
1. Compare `p_adj` versus `p_role` specifically on `role_ctx_on` rows.
2. Trace how those rows flow into slip ranking so we can see whether the bottleneck is probability quality or selection logic.

Searched for regex `teamshare_rel_effective_threshold|rotation_reweight_enabled|min_games_for_pattern|capture_rate|role_guardrails` (`**/src/Atlas/model/team_share_reallocator.py`), 42 results

Searched for regex `weight_mode|blend_alpha|usage_power|rotation_last_n_games|min_rotation_games|min_rotation_avg_min` (`**/src/Atlas/model/team_share_reallocator.py`), 33 results

Yes. The clean way to think about it is:

- `p_adj` vs `p_role` is a surface comparison.
- `teamshare` allocator and share-matrix building are upstream signal construction.

So I would not mix those together when explaining or changing the model.

**What the teamshare layer actually does**
- The share matrix is built in team_share_reallocator.py. It learns historical beneficiary weights from gamelogs.
- The allocator then turns outs into a weight vector in team_share_reallocator.py.
- The final role multiplier comes from team_share_reallocator.py, which mixes:
- teamshare weights
- usage weights
- rotation reweight
- depth-based capture
- guardrails/caps

That means there are two very different kinds of changes:

1. **Build share matrix changes**
- Change what historical rows are eligible.
- Change how “rotation players” are defined.
- Change `recent_days`, `min_rotation_games`, or `min_rotation_avg_min`.
- Change which outs get represented at all.

This is data-shaping. It changes the learned support the allocator sees.

2. **Allocator logic changes**
- Change how outs are blended into beneficiary weights.
- Change `weight_mode`, `blend_alpha`, `usage_power`, or rotation reweight behavior.
- Change the reliability handling around sparse patterns.
- Change caps or guardrails like `role_guardrails_*` or `capture_rate`.

This is model behavior. It changes how the same share matrix is interpreted.

**Why I think it is inconsistent**
The current code already tells me the instability is coming from the interaction of support and context:
- `build_teamshare_weights_for_outs` now uses a smooth reliability factor instead of a hard cutoff.
- `compute_role_multiplier` still falls back through multiple layers if the share matrix is weak.
- `role_guardrails_*` can clip the final multiplier when the removed budget gets large.

So if the backtest is noisy, the likely cause is not just one bad coefficient. It is usually one of these:
- the share matrix is too sparse or too broad
- the rotation set is too permissive
- the blend is too eager on weak patterns
- the guardrails are compensating for noisy upstream weights

**How I would approach it**
- First compare `p_adj` and `p_role` on the rows where `role_ctx_outs_used > 0`.
- If `p_role` is already worse there, the problem is upstream in the teamshare/role-context layer.
- If `p_role` is better and `p_adj` is worse, then the blowout/minutes adjustment is the issue.
- If both are weak, then the share matrix or allocator logic needs tightening.

**What I would call out before changing anything**
If I decide to change code in this area, I’ll explicitly label it as one of:
- “share matrix construction change”
- “teamshare allocator change”
- “role-context probability change”
- “telemetry calibration change”

That way we do not confuse a data-scope change with a modeling change.

**Practical read on your current setup**
Given what I’ve seen:
- `role_ctx_off` is the cleaner calibration surface.
- `role_ctx_on` is the fragile branch.
- The teamshare allocator is the most likely place for inconsistency, because it is where sparse historical share patterns get turned into actual probability movement.

So yes, I agree with you: that file is the right place to tighten up if we decide the instability is upstream rather than in calibration.

If you want, next I can do the `p_adj` vs `p_role` comparison specifically for `role_ctx_on` rows and show you whether the allocator is the source of the drift.

User: So the reason it is either tight or weak is because if a player is out who produces very little then the gain to the other players will be weak bc that players output is little. Some are harsh because if a star player is out and a lot of stats need to be redistributed than it floods. Some teams are deeper than others so it affects more players and some teams are weaker and it affects fewer players. That is the logic behind the math and it has worked out for my slips however if it is throwing off the rest of the model than I am open to changes but the philosophical reasoning has to make sense as well as the math

GitHub Copilot: Your philosophy is coherent.

The intuition is:

- A weak player being out should create a small redistribution because there is not much production to reallocate.
- A star being out should create a larger redistribution because a lot of usage and counting stats need to land somewhere else.
- Deep teams should spread that gain across more beneficiaries.
- Thin teams should concentrate the gain into fewer players.

That is exactly the kind of behavior a teamshare allocator should try to capture, and it is a good basketball-native model of the problem.

The important distinction is this:

- The philosophy can be right.
- The implementation can still be too noisy, too sparse, or too sensitive.

That is the real risk in src/Atlas/model/team_share_reallocator.py. The current allocator is trying to express your philosophy through:
- removed budget
- share-matrix beneficiary weights
- usage blending
- rotation reweighting
- depth capture
- guardrails and caps

So if the math is throwing off the rest of the model, I would not throw out the philosophy. I would tighten the implementation so the philosophy is expressed more cleanly.

What I think is the right mental model:

- Size of injury matters.
- Team depth matters.
- Historical support matters.
- Redistribution should be proportional, not arbitrary.
- Sparse evidence should shrink the effect, not eliminate the idea.

That means the allocator should behave like this:
- big star out -> larger redistribution
- small bench out -> small redistribution
- well-supported patterns -> stronger confidence
- weakly supported patterns -> softer effect
- deep roster -> wider spread
- shallow roster -> tighter spread

Where this can go wrong is if the allocator overuses a weak share pattern and turns a small injury into a big synthetic bump, or if it spreads a star injury too broadly and floods players who should not gain much.

So my recommendation is:

- Keep the philosophy.
- Tighten the math around it.
- Make the allocator more explicit about three things:
  - removed budget
  - support strength
  - redistribution concentration

That preserves the basketball logic while reducing model drift.

In practical terms, the places that correspond to that philosophy are:
- share matrix construction in team_share_reallocator.py
- beneficiary weighting in team_share_reallocator.py
- final multiplier formation in team_share_reallocator.py

If we change anything, I would keep it philosophy-preserving:
- no change to the idea that star outs should redistribute more
- no change to the idea that depth changes the spread
- yes to better smoothing, stronger support gating, and clearer caps

So the short answer is: your reasoning makes sense, and I would only change the allocator if the math is not respecting that reasoning cleanly enough.

If you want, I can next do one of two things:
1. Map that philosophy directly onto the current allocator knobs so you can see which parameters control each part.
2. Propose a tighter version of the allocator logic that keeps the same basketball meaning but reduces instability.

User: Yes, do both of those

GitHub Copilot: Yes. Here is the clean mapping, and then the tighter version I would recommend.

**Knob Map**

| Philosophy | Current implementation knobs | What they do |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Weak player out means small gain | capture_rate in team_share_reallocator.py, role_guardrails_removed_threshold in team_share_reallocator.py | Limits how much removed budget gets redistributed at all |
| Star out means bigger gain | capture_rate, depth_capture_enabled, depth_slope, role_guardrails_cap_mult | Lets large removed budgets create larger bumps, but still caps extreme results |
| Deep team spreads more | build_removed_share_matrix in team_share_reallocator.py, especially recent_days, min_rotation_games, min_rotation_avg_min | More stable rotation players means more plausible beneficiaries |
| Thin team concentrates more | same builder plus rotation_reweight_enabled in team_share_reallocator.py | Fewer credible beneficiaries, so gain concentrates |
| Strong historical support should count more | build_teamshare_weights_for_outs in team_share_reallocator.py, min_games_for_pattern, teamshare_rel_effective_threshold, alpha_mult | Weak patterns get shrunk, strong patterns get trusted |
| Sparse evidence should stay soft | weight_mode, blend_alpha, usage_power, rotation_reweight_enabled | Blends usage fallback with teamshare so weak patterns do not dominate |
| Final gain should not flood the model | depth_cap_min, depth_cap_max, depth_conc_enabled, role_guardrails_cap_mult, role_guardrails_softness | Keeps the final multiplier from becoming too aggressive |

A plain-language read of the code is:

- build_removed_share_matrix learns who benefits when someone is missing.
- build_teamshare_weights_for_outs turns that history into beneficiary weights for the current out list.
- compute_role_multiplier decides how much of the removed budget gets redistributed and to whom.
- p_role is then carried downstream, while p_adj is the later blowout/minutes adjustment in new_probability.py and new_probability.py.

So your philosophy is already represented in the math: bigger injury, bigger budget; deeper team, broader spread; weaker evidence, softer effect.

**Tighter Design**

What I would tighten, while keeping the same basketball logic, is not the philosophy but the separation of concerns.

- Removed budget should answer: how much value left the injured player?
- Support should answer: how much do we trust the historical redistribution pattern?
- Concentration should answer: how many teammates should receive the gain?

That gives you a cleaner model than mixing all three into one blended multiplier.

Concretely, I would keep the same direction but make the following adjustments:

- Keep build_removed_share_matrix as the evidence builder, but make its roster eligibility stricter if the pool is noisy.
- Keep build_teamshare_weights_for_outs as the beneficiary allocator, but make weak support decay faster than strong support.
- Keep compute_role_multiplier as the final assembly point, but make the final bump depend more explicitly on confidence and concentration.
- Keep the guardrails, but treat them as a safety layer, not the main logic.

The version I would aim for is:

- star out -> larger removed budget
- bench out -> smaller removed budget
- strong support -> trust the share matrix more
- weak support -> fall back more toward usage
- deep team -> spread wider
- thin team -> spread narrower
- final result -> capped, but not flattened

**What I Would Change If We Touch Logic**

If I change something in this area, I would label it clearly as one of these:

- Share matrix construction change
- Teamshare allocator change
- Role-context probability change
- Calibration overlay change

That matters because each one has a different meaning:

- Share matrix construction change means we changed what historical patterns the model learns from.
- Teamshare allocator change means we changed how those patterns turn into p_role.
- Calibration overlay change means we only changed the final probability surface, not the teamshare logic.

For your question, the likely place to tighten is the teamshare allocator, not calibration.

**My recommendation**

- Keep the philosophy exactly as it is.
- Tighten the implementation so the model is less sensitive to weak share patterns.
- Make support, concentration, and removed budget more explicit.
- Do not collapse the teamshare idea into calibration; that would lose the basketball meaning you want.

If you want, I can next do one of these two things:

1. Propose a concrete v3 teamshare design with exact logic changes, but no code yet.
2. Compare p_adj versus p_role on the role_ctx_on rows and show whether the allocator is actually the source of the drift.

- The redistributed budget now gets support-shrunk before it is spread:

- That logic is in team_share_reallocator.py and team_share_reallocator.py.

GitHub Copilot: That matches the pipeline.

So the working rule is:
- live runs produce scored and recommendation outputs first
- eval_legs.csv is only available after the next-day backtest reconstructs outcomes from gamelogs plus the PrizePicks board data
- the telemetry and audit folders are where we compare model variants across completed days

That means the right place for hit-based validation is the backtest/audit corpus, not the current live run root. The earlier 2026-03-17 reconstruction was valid for that purpose; the 2026-03-18 live run cannot be scored yet because the outcomes have not landed.

pd.to_numeric(eval_df['hit'], errors='coerce')\n    role = pd.to_numeric(eval_df['role_ctx_outs_used'], errors='coerce').fillna(0)\n    mask = y.notna()\n    if not mask.any():\n        continue\n    def metric(col, subset_mask=None):\n        p = pd.to_numeric(eval_df[col], errors='coerce').clip(1e-6, 1-1e-6)\n        m = mask if subset_mask is None else (mask & subset_mask)\n        if not m.any():\n            return np.nan, np.nan\n        return float(((p[m]-y[m])**2).mean()), float((-(y[m]*np.log(p[m]) + (1-y[m])*np.log(1-p[m]))).mean())\n    off = role <= 0\n    on = role > 0\n    b_adj, ll_adj = metric('p_adj')\n    b_role, ll_role = metric('p_role')\n    b_cal, ll_cal = metric('p_cal')\n    b_adj_on, ll_adj_on = metric('p_adj', on)\n    b_role_on, ll_role_on = metric('p_role', on)\n    b_adj_off, ll_adj_off = metric('p_adj', off)\n    b_role_off, ll_role_off = metric('p_role', off)\n    records.append({\n        'run_dir': str(run_dir.relative_to(root)),\n        'family': run_dir.parts[-3] if len(run_dir.parts) >= 3 else run_dir.name,\n        'rows': len(eval_df),\n        'labeled': int(mask.sum()),\n        'role_on_share': float(on.mean()),\n        'brier_adj': b_adj,\n        'brier_role': b_role,\n        'brier_cal': b_cal,\n        'll_adj': ll_adj,\n        'll_role': ll_role,\n        'll_cal': ll_cal,\n        'brier_delta_role_minus_adj': b_role - b_adj,\n        'll_delta_role_minus_adj': ll_role - ll_adj,\n        'brier_delta_role_minus_adj_on': b_role_on - b_adj_on,\n        'brier_delta_role_minus_adj_off': b_role_off - b_adj_off,\n        'll_delta_role_minus_adj_on': ll_role_on - ll_adj_on,\n        'll_delta_role_minus_adj_off': ll_role_off - ll_adj_off,\n    })\n\ndf = pd.DataFrame(records)\nprint('\\nTop families by total labeled rows')\nfamily_summary = df.groupby('family', as_index=False).agg(rows=('rows','sum'), labeled=('labeled','sum'), brier_adj=('brier_adj', lambda s: np.nan), brier_role=('brier_role', lambda s: np.nan))\n# compute weighted metrics manually\nsummaries = []\nfor fam, grp in df.groupby('family'):\n    total_labeled = grp['labeled'].sum()\n    summaries.append({\n        'family': fam,\n        'runs': len(grp),\n        'rows': int(grp['rows'].sum()),\n        'labeled': int(total_labeled),\n        'avg_role_on_share': float(np.average(grp['role_on_share'], weights=grp['labeled'])),\n        'weighted_brier_adj': float(np.average(grp['brier_adj'], weights=grp['labeled'])),\n        'weighted_brier_role': float(np.average(grp['brier_role'], weights=grp['labeled'])),\n        'weighted_brier_cal': float(np.average(grp['brier_cal'], weights=grp['labeled'])),\n        'weighted_ll_adj': float(np.average(grp['ll_adj'], weights=grp['labeled'])),\n        'weighted_ll_role': float(np.average(grp['ll_role'], weights=grp['labeled'])),\n        'weighted_ll_cal': float(np.average(grp['ll_cal'], weights=grp['labeled'])),\n        'weighted_brier_delta': float(np.average(grp['brier_delta_role_minus_adj'], weights=grp['labeled'])),\n        'weighted_ll_delta': float(np.average(grp['ll_delta_role_minus_adj'], weights=grp['labeled'])),\n        'weighted_brier_delta_on': float(np.average(grp['brier_delta_role_minus_adj_on'], weights=grp['labeled'])),\n        'weighted_brier_delta_off': float(np.average(grp['brier_delta_role_minus_adj_off'], weights=grp['labeled'])),\n    })\nsummary = pd.DataFrame(summaries).sort_values(['labeled','rows'], ascending=False)\nprint(summary.head(12).to_string(index=False))\n\nbiggest = df.sort_values('labeled', ascending=False).head(10)\nprint('\\nLargest control runs')\nprint(biggest[['run_dir','labeled','rows','role_on_share','brier_adj','brier_role','brier_delta_role_minus_adj']].to_string(index=False))\n"

**Findings**
- The completed control corpus is consistent: across 146 control runs and 463,815 labeled rows, p_role is ahead of p_adj overall.
- Overall control metrics were:
  - all rows: p_adj Brier 0.211020, p_role 0.210695, p_cal 0.210744
  - role_off rows: p_adj 0.211188, p_role 0.211099
  - role_on rows: p_adj 0.210301, p_role 0.208957
- The biggest control families all point the same way. The strongest improvements were in telemetry_ab_control and under_relief_ab_control, with baseline_standard_control_20260310 also slightly positive. See the rollup behavior in baseline_standard_control_20260310 and the broader corpus summary in telemetry_corpus_reader.py.
- On the largest control run, outputtelem/baseline_standard_control_20260310/runs/20260310_135848/eval_legs.csv, the role-on slice still helped:
  - all rows: p_adj Brier 0.203261, p_role 0.203164, p_cal 0.203102
  - role_on rows: p_adj 0.182080, p_role 0.181393
- The weakest role-on stat slices in that run were FG3M, REB, AST, and PTS. The weakest stat-direction combos were FG3M OVER and REB OVER. A few low-probability rows were still worse under p_role than p_adj, but the regressions were narrow, not broad.
- The earlier live-run correction still stands: live folders only write scored and recommendation outputs, while eval_legs.csv belongs to completed backtests or reconstruction passes.

**Pipeline**
- The backtest flow is:
  - raw PrizePicks JSON goes into the replay rebuild
  - rebuild_today.py rebuilds the today board from that payload
  - backtest_v2.py applies replay mode and started-game gating
  - it writes today.csv, snapshots, and then eval_legs.csv after merging against truth logs
- The live publish flow only writes scored_legs.csv and scored_legs_deduped.csv, which is why you only see eval artifacts in backtest and audit folders. See publish_run_outputs.py.

**Bottom Line**
- The control corpus supports keeping p_role as the better branch.
- The remaining weakness is not a broad failure, it is concentrated in a small set of stat families, especially FG3M and REB on role-on rows.
- The pipeline interpretation you gave is correct, and it explains why eval_legs.csv appears only after the next-day backtest pass.

If you want, I can next do one of these:
1. Pull the worst FG3M and REB role-on rows across the control corpus and see whether the allocator or calibration is the cause.
2. Compare the strongest control family against the closest candidate family, run by run, on the same dates.
3. Inspect whether the replay gating in rebuild_today.py is dropping anything you care about before backtest.

Completed: *Find worst role-on slices* (1/3)

1. Weak FG3M / REB slices
- The worst role-on rows are tiny-probability tails, not broad allocator failures. The recurring examples are Dorian Finney-Smith FG3M OVER 2.5, Isaac Okoro REB OVER 6.5, Nique Clifford FG3M OVER 3.5, Reed Sheppard REB OVER 6.5, and Max Christie FG3M OVER 6.5.
- In the control corpus, FG3M and REB still improve under p_role across every major control family I checked. For example, eval_legs.csv has FG3M role-on Brier 0.195181 vs 0.196009 and REB 0.200540 vs 0.201680.
- That pattern says the weak spots are mostly calibration-tail noise on low-confidence rows, not a broken share allocator.

2. Control vs candidate on matched ladders
- The cleanest same-date comparison is eval_legs.csv vs eval_legs.csv. They are essentially tied on p_adj and p_role to 6 decimals, and the challenger does not beat the control.
- On that ladder, overall p_role is still better than p_adj, but the role-on slice itself is a tiny regression in both families. That matches the earlier diagnosis that the role-on branch is the fragile part.
- The secondary pair, eval_legs.csv vs eval_legs.csv, is similar. The control is slightly better on p_adj, p_role is effectively tied, and the challenger is not a step forward.
- Net: the closest challenger families confirm the same behavior, but they do not improve on control in a material way.

3. Replay gating
- The replay rebuild itself does not apply the live current-time filter in replay mode. See rebuild_today.py.
- The backtest layer then applies the started-game gate before writing today.csv and eval_legs.csv. See backtest_v2.py and the eval write path in backtest_v2.py.
- The gate builds started_ids from payload game start times and drops any rows whose game_id has already started at the cutoff. If cutoff data or game start data is missing, the gate is skipped.
- So yes, backtest can and does drop started games before eval; that is expected and is part of why live runs do not have eval_legs yet.

Bottom line: p_role is still the better branch overall, the candidate ladders are not beating control, and the remaining weakness is concentrated in a few FG3M and REB role-on tails.

If you want, I can next drill into the exact FG3M and REB rows that regress under p_role and group them by role_ctx features so we can see whether this is coming from the allocator or the calibration overlay.

  "codeSnippet": "import pandas as pd\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\npath = Path(r'c:\\Users\\rick\\projects\\Atlas\\outputtelem\\under_relief_single\\runs\\20260315_164053\\eval_legs.csv')\ndf = pd.read_csv(path)\nprint('rows', len(df))\nprint('cols', len(df.columns))\nfor col in ['role_ctx_reason','role_ctx_components','role_ctx_component_reasons','role_ctx_outs_used','teamshare_support_budget_mult','role_ctx_mult','p_role','p_adj','actual']:\n    print(col, col in df.columns)\n\n# Basic comparison where both p_role and p_adj exist\nif {'p_role','p_adj','actual'}.issubset(df.columns):\n    for label, mask in [('all', pd.Series([True]*len(df))), ('role_on', df.get('role_ctx_outs_used', 0).fillna(0) > 0), ('role_off', df.get('role_ctx_outs_used', 0).fillna(0) == 0)]:\n        sub = df.loc[mask].copy()\n        if len(sub) == 0:\n            continue\n        if {'actual','p_role','p_adj'}.issubset(sub.columns):\n            brier_role = ((sub['p_role'] - yy)**2).mean()\n        brier_adj = ((sub['p_adj'] - yy)**2).mean()\n        agg.append((reason, len(sub), brier_role, brier_adj, brier_role - brier_adj))\n    reason_df = pd.DataFrame(agg, columns=['reason','n','brier_role','brier_adj','delta']).sort_values(['n','delta'], ascending=[False, True])\n    print('\\nReason summary:')\n    print(reason_df.head(20).to_string(index=False))\n\n# rows where role worsens brier the most\nbrier_delta = (df['p_role'] - Completed with input: {
  "workspaceRoot": "c:\\Users\\rick\\projects\\Atlas",
  "codeSnippet": "import json\nimport math\nimport numpy as np\nimport pandas as pd\nimport yaml\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\n\ndef soft_cap_multiplier(m, cap, softness=0.35):\n    try:\n        m = float(m)\n        c = float(cap)\n        s = float(softness)\n    except Exception:\n        return m\n    if not np.isfinite(m) or not np.isfinite(c) or c <= 0:\n        return m\n    if m <= c:\n        return m\n    s = float(np.clip(s, 0.0, 1.0))\n    return float(c + s * (m - c))\n\n# paths\ncfg_path = Path(\"config.telemetry_schema_v2.yaml\")\ncsv_path = Path(r\"outputtelem/under_relief_single/runs/20260315_164053/eval_legs.csv\")\n\nif not cfg_path.exists():\n    raise FileNotFoundError(f\"config not found: {cfg_path}\")\nif not csv_path.exists():\n    raise FileNotFoundError(f\"csv not found: {csv_path}\")\n\n# load config\nwith open(cfg_path, \"r\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as f:\n    cfg = yaml.safe_load(f)\n\nguard = (cfg.get(\"allocator\") or {}).get(\"role_guardrails\") or {}\nmin_outs = int(guard.get(\"min_outs\", 2) or 2)\ncap_mult = guard.get(\"cap_mult\") or {}\nsoftness = float(guard.get(\"softness\", 0.15) or 0.15)\n\n# load csv\ndf = pd.read_csv(csv_path, low_memory=False)\n# coerce important cols\nfor c in [\"role_ctx_outs_used\", \"role_ctx_mult_raw\", \"role_ctx_mult\", \"p_adj\", \"p_role\"]:\n    if c in df.columns:\n        df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors='coerce')\n    else:\n        df[c] = np.nan\n\nif 'hit' not in df.columns:\n    df['hit'] = 0\nelse:\n    df['hit'] = pd.to_numeric(df['hit'], errors='coerce').fillna(0).astype(int)\n\nif 'stat' not in df.columns:\n    df['stat'] = ''\n\ndf['stat_u'] = df['stat'].astype(str).str.upper().str.strip()\n\n# compute new multiplier per row\n\ndef compute_new_mult(row):\n    stat = row['stat_u']\n    raw = row.get('role_ctx_mult_raw')\n    outs = int(row.get('role_ctx_outs_used') or 0)\n    cap = cap_mult.get(stat)\n    # if no raw multiplier, fallback to existing final\n    if raw is None or (isinstance(raw, float) and np.isnan(raw)):\n        return float(row.get('role_ctx_mult') or 1.0)\n    try:\n        rawf = float(raw)\n    except Exception:\n        return float(row.get('role_ctx_mult') or 1.0)\n    if cap is None:\n        return float(row.get('role_ctx_mult') or rawf)\n    try:\n        capf = float(cap)\n    except Exception:\n        return float(row.get('role_ctx_mult') or rawf)\n    # guardrail triggers on multi-out (we cannot reliably access removed_budget here)\n    if outs >= min_outs:\n        return soft_cap_multiplier(rawf, capf, softness)\n    # otherwise no change\n    return float(row.get('role_ctx_mult') or rawf)\n\n# apply\ndf['new_mult'] = df.apply(compute_new_mult, axis=1)\n# new p_role\n# if p_adj missing, fall back to p\nif 'p_adj' not in df.columns or df['p_adj'].isna().all():\n    if 'p' in df.columns:\n        df['p_adj'] = pd.to_numeric(df['p'], errors='coerce')\n    else:\n        df['p_adj'] = 0.0\n\n# ensure p_role exists\nif 'p_role' not in df.columns or df['p_role'].isna().all():\n    df['p_role'] = df['p_adj'] * df.get('role_ctx_mult', 1.0)\n\n# compute new p_role\n df['p_role_new'] = df['p_adj'].fillna(0.0) * df['new_mult']\n\n# metrics\n\ndef brier(preds, actuals):\n    a = np.asarray(preds)\n    b = np.asarray(actuals)\n    mask = ~np.isnan(a)\n    if not mask.any():\n        return None\n    return float(np.nanmean((a[mask] - b[mask]) ** 2))\n\nmask_role_on = (df['role_ctx_outs_used'].fillna(0) > 0)\nmask_guardrail = ((df['role_ctx_outs_used'].fillna(0) >= min_outs) & df['role_ctx_mult_raw'].notna())\n\nmetrics = {\n    'n_total': int(len(df)),\n    'n_role_on': int(mask_role_on.sum()),\n    'n_guardrail': int(mask_guardrail.sum()),\n    'brier_role_old_all': brier(df['p_role'], df['hit']),\n    'brier_role_new_all': brier(df['p_role_new'], df['hit']),\n    'brier_role_old_role_on': brier(df.loc[mask_role_on, 'p_role'], df.loc[mask_role_on, 'hit']) if mask_role_on.any() else None,\n    'brier_role_new_role_on': brier(df.loc[mask_role_on, 'p_role_new'], df.loc[mask_role_on, c))\n\n\ndef brier(preds, actuals):\n    a = np.asarray(preds)\n    b = np.asarray(actuals)\n    mask = ~np.isnan(a)\n    if not mask.any():\n        return None\n    return float(np.nanmean((a[mask] - b[mask]) ** 2))\n\nresults = {}\nfor alpha in [0.0, 0.5, 1.0]:\n    old_support = old_floor + (1.0 - old_floor) * alpha\n    new_support = new_floor + (1.0 - new_floor) * alpha\n    ratio = (new_support / old_support) if old_support > 0 else 1.0\n\n    new_mults = []\n    for _, r in df.iterrows():\n        stat = r['stat_u']\n        outs = int(r.get('role_ctx_outs_used') or 0)\n        raw = r.get('role_ctx_mult_raw')\n        if not np.isfinite(raw):\n            raw = r.get('role_ctx_mult')\n        if not np.isfinite(raw):\n            raw = 1.0\n        try:\n            rawf = float(raw)\n        except Exception:\n            rawf = 1.0\n        # scale redistributed portion\n        new_raw = 1.0 + (rawf - 1.0) * ratio\n        # apply guardrail if outs >= min_outs\n        cap = cap_mult.get(stat, None)\n        if outs >= min_outs and cap is not None:\n            try:\n                capf = float(cap)\n                new_raw = soft_cap_multiplier(new_raw, capf, softness)\n            except Exception:\n                pass\n        # clamp to reasonable max\n        new_raw = min(new_raw, 2.2)\n        new_mults.append(new_raw)\n\n    df['p_role_sim_{}'.format(int(alpha*100))] = df['p_adj'].fillna(0.0) * np.array(new_mults)\n\n    mask_role_on = (df['role_ctx_outs_used'].fillna(0) > 0)\n    mask_guardrail = (df['role_ctx_outs_used'].fillna(0) >= min_outs) & df['role_ctx_mult_raw'].notna()\n\n    metrics = {\n        'alpha': alpha,\n        'n_total': int(len(df)),\n        'n_role_on': int(mask_role_on.sum()),\n        'n_guardrail': int(mask_guardrail.sum()),\n        'brier_role_old_all': brier(df['p_role'], df['hit']),\n        'brier_role_new_all': brier(df['p_role_sim_{}'.format(int(alpha*100))], df['hit']),\n        'brier_role_old_role_on': brier(df.loc[mask_role_on, 'p_role'], df.loc[mask_role_on, 'hit']) if mask_role_on.any() else None,\n        'brier_role_new_role_on': brier(df.loc[mask_role_on, 'p_role_sim_{}'.format(int(alpha*100))], df.loc[mask_role_on, 'hit']) if mask_role_on.any() else None,\n        'brier_guardrail_old': brier(df.loc[mask_guardrail, 'p_role'], df.loc[mask_guardrail, 'hit']) if mask_guardrail.any() else None,\n        'brier_guardrail_new': brier(df.loc[mask_guardrail, 'p_role_sim_{}'.format(int(alpha*100))], df.loc[mask_guardrail, 'hit']) if mask_guardrail.any() else None,\n        'old_support': old_support,\n        'new_support': new_support,\n        'ratio': ratio,\n    }\n    results[str(alpha)] = metrics\n\nprint(json.dumps(results, indent=2))\n",

  "codeSnippet": "import json\nimport numpy as np\nimport pandas as pd\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\ncsv_path = Path(r\"outputtelem/under_relief_single/runs/20260315_164053/eval_legs.csv\")\ndf = pd.read_csv(csv_path, low_memory=False)\nfor c in ['p_adj','p_role','hit','role_ctx_outs_used','role_ctx_mult','role_ctx_mult_raw','usg_proxy']:\n    if c in df.columns:\n        df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors='coerce')\n    else:\n        df[c] = np.nan\n\nif 'hit' in df.columns:\n    df['hit'] = pd.to_numeric(df['hit'], errors='coerce').fillna(0).astype(int)\nelse:\n    df['hit'] = 0\n\nmask_role_on = df['role_ctx_outs_used'].fillna(0) > 0\nmask_ok_combo = df['role_ctx_reason'].astype(str).eq('ok_combo') if 'role_ctx_reason' in df.columns else pd.Series(False, index=df.index)\n\nbase_mult = df['role_ctx_mult'].fillna(df['role_ctx_mult_raw']).fillna(1.0).to_numpy(dtype=float)\nusage = df['usg_proxy'].fillna(df['usg_proxy'].median()).to_numpy(dtype=float)\nmedian_usage = float(np.nanmedian(usage)) if np.isfinite(usage).any() else 1.0\nmedian_usage = max(median_usage, 1e-6)\nusage_ratio = np.clip(usage / median_usage, 0.25, 4.0)\n\n\ndef brier(preds, actuals):\n    a = np.asarray(preds, dtype=float)\n    b = np.asarray(actuals, dtype=float)\n    mask = ~np.isnan(a)\n    return float(np.nanmean((a[mask] - b[mask]) ** 2))\n\nbaseline_all = brier(df['p_role'], df['hit'])\nbaseline_role_on = brier(df.loc[mask_role_on, 'p_role'], df.loc[mask_role_on, 'hit'])\nbaseline_ok_combo = brier(df.loc[mask_ok_combo, 'p_role'], df.loc[mask_ok_combo, 'hit']) if mask_ok_combo.any() else None\n\nrows = []\nfor g in [0.85, 0.90, 0.95, 1.00, 1.05]:\n    for delta in [0.0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, 1.25]:\n        sim_mult = 1.0 + g * (base_mult - 1.0) * (usage_ratio ** delta)\n        sim_mult = np.clip(sim_mult, 0.0, 2.2)\n        sim = df['p_adj'].fillna(0.0).to_numpy(dtype=float) * sim_mult\n        all_b = brier(sim, df['hit'])\n        role_b = brier(sim[mask_role_on.to_numpy()], df.loc[mask_role_on, 'hit']) if mask_role_on.any() else np.nan\n        ok_b = brier(sim[mask_ok_combo.to_numpy()], df.loc[mask_ok_combo, 'hit']) if mask_ok_combo.any() else np.nan\n        score = (all_b - baseline_all) + 0.75 * max(0.0, role_b - baseline_role_on) + 0.50 * max(0.0, (ok_b - baseline_ok_combo) if baseline_ok_combo is not None else 0.0)\n        rows.append({\n            'g': g,\n            'delta': delta,\n            'all_brier': all_b,\n            'role_on_brier': role_b,\n            'ok_combo_brier': ok_b,\n            'score': score,\n            'mean_role_prob': float(np.nanmean(sim)),\n        })\n\nbest_by_score = sorted(rows, key=lambda r: r['score'])[:10]\nprint(json.dumps({\n    'baseline': {\n        'all_brier': baseline_all,\n        'role_on_brier': baseline_role_on,\n        'ok_combo_brier': baseline_ok_combo,\n        'mean_role_prob': float(np.nanmean(df['p_role'])),\n    },\n    'top10': best_by_score,\n}, indent=2))\n",
  "workingDirectory": "c:\\Users\\rick\\projects\\Atlas",
  "timeout": 120000
}

Ran `pylanceRunCodeSnippet` 
Completed with input: {
  "workspaceRoot": "c:\\Users\\rick\\projects\\Atlas",
  "codeSnippet": "import json\nimport numpy as np\nimport pandas as pd\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\ncsv_path = Path(r\"outputtelem/under_relief_single/runs/20260315_164053/eval_legs.csv\")\ndf = pd.read_csv(csv_path, low_memory=False)\nfor c in ['p_adj','p_role','hit','role_ctx_outs_used','role_ctx_mult','role_ctx_mult_raw','usg_proxy']:\n    if c in df.columns:\n        df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors='coerce')\n    else:\n        df[c] = np.nan\n\nif 'hit' in df.columns:\n    df['hit'] = pd.to_numeric(df['hit'], errors='coerce').fillna(0).astype(int)\nelse:\n    df['hit'] = 0\n\nmask_role_on = df['role_ctx_outs_used'].fillna(0) > 0\nmask_ok_combo = df['role_ctx_reason'].astype(str).eq('ok_combo') if 'role_ctx_reason' in df.columns else pd.Series(False, index=df.index)\n\nbase_mult = df['role_ctx_mult'].fillna(df['role_ctx_mult_raw']).fillna(1.0).to_numpy(dtype=float)\nusage = df['usg_proxy'].fillna(df['usg_proxy'].median()).to_numpy(dtype=float)\nmedian_usage = float(np.nanmedian(usage)) if np.isfinite(usage).any() else 1.0\nmedian_usage = max(median_usage, 1e-6)\nusage_ratio = np.clip(usage / median_usage, 0.25, 4.0)\n\n\ndef brier(preds, actuals):\n    a = np.asarray(preds, dtype=float)\n    b = np.asarray(actuals, dtype=float)\n    mask = ~np.isnan(a)\n    return float(np.nanmean((a[mask] - b[mask]) ** 2))\n\nbaseline = {\n    'all': brier(df['p_role'], df['hit']),\n    'role_on': brier(df.loc[mask_role_on, 'p_role'], df.loc[mask_role_on, 'hit']),\n    'ok_combo': brier(df.loc[mask_ok_combo, 'p_role'], df.loc[mask_ok_combo, 'hit']) if mask_ok_combo.any() else np.nan,\n}\n\nweights = {'all': 0.50, 'role_on': 0.30, 'ok_combo': 0.20}\nrows = []\nfor g in [0.80, 0.85, 0.90, 0.95, 1.00]:\n    for delta in [0.75, 1.00, 1.25, 1.50]:\n        sim_mult = 1.0 + g * (base_mult - 1.0) * (usage_ratio ** delta)\n        sim_mult = np.clip(sim_mult, 0.0, 2.2)\n        sim = df['p_adj'].fillna(0.0).to_numpy(dtype=float) * sim_mult\n        metrics = {\n            'all': brier(sim, df['hit']),\n            'role_on': brier(sim[mask_role_on.to_numpy()], df.loc[mask_role_on, 'hit']),\n            'ok_combo': brier(sim[mask_ok_combo.to_numpy()], df.loc[mask_ok_combo, 'hit']) if mask_ok_combo.any() else np.nan,\n        }\n        weighted = sum(weights[k] * metrics[k] for k in weights)\n        rows.append({\n            'g': g,\n            'delta': delta,\n            'all_brier': metrics['all'],\n            'role_on_brier': metrics['role_on'],\n            'ok_combo_brier': metrics['ok_combo'],\n            'weighted': weighted,\n        })\n\nrows = sorted(rows, key=lambda r: r['weighted'])\nprint(json.dumps({'baseline': baseline, 'top10': rows[:10]}, indent=2))\n",
  "workingDirectory": "c:\\Users\\rick\\projects\\Atlas",
  "timeout": 120000
}

= pd.read_csv(p, low_memory=False)\n# normalize key columns if present\nfor c in ['team','out_player','beneficiary_player','stat','weight','games']:\n    if c not in df.columns:\n        raise ValueError(f'missing column {c}; have {list(df.columns)[:20]}')\n\nfor c in ['weight','games']:\n    df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors='coerce')\n\n# basic shape and support sparsity\nsummary = {\n float(pat['games_max'].quantile(q)) for q in [0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.9, 0.95]},\n}\n\n# estimate support floor threshold impact using current min_games_for_pattern=8 logic\nfor thr in [3, 5, 8, 10, 12]:\n    rel = np.minimum(1.0, pat['games_max'].fillna(0) / float(thr))\n    support_stats[f'rel_mean_thr_{thr}'] = float(rel.mean())\n    support_stats[f'rel_lt_0.25_thr_{thr}'] = float((rel < Ran `pylanceRunCodeSnippet` 
Completed with input: {
  "workspaceRoot": "c:\\Users\\rick\\projects\\Atlas",
